rationale: The citing paper uses Darke and Ritchie (2007) in its theory development for Study 2 as direct evidence that suspicion/deception can shape later ad processing and distrust across sources. It is not just listed in background; the authors rely on it to motivate their suspicion-prime manipulation and expected effects on persuasion knowledge. In the General Discussion, they explicitly position their contribution relative to Darke and Ritchie by arguing they show a broader spillover effect from non-advertising corporate-fraud cues. This indicates a meaningful build-on relationship.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Darke and Ritchie (2007) as prior evidence of deception-driven distrust spillover, then broadens that line by showing similar vigilance can be triggered by more general suspicion cues and by linking the effect to regulatory focus.
